text: Atomistic simulations are widely used in drug design, catalysis, soft matter physics and solid state physics, to name a few. With the advancement of machine learning, interatomic potentials (IAPs) based on machine learning (ML) approach, namely, machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs), have attracted increasing attention due to the fact that they are much faster than ab initio methods and still retain good accuracy. An universal (or general purpose) reactive MLIP that does not need to be re-trained for different systems is always highly desired. In this work, we report a general-purpose reactive MLIP, ANI-1xnr, which is constructed by learning potentials from the data generated by the automated sampling of chemical reactions in condensed phases. We then apply ANI-1xnr to model five distinct reactions, involving solid-phase carbon nucleation, the formation of graphene rings from acetylene, biofuel additives degradation, methane combustion, and prebiotic formation of glycine from small molecules. The results are compared with experimental data and other theoretical results, demonstrating the accuracy and applicability of ANI-1xnr. We conclude that ANI-1xnr can be used as a general-purpose model to study C, H, N and O elements at the atomistic scale, which can pave the way for high-throughput simulations of reactive systems in different fields.
